coredata.dc:title: N-doped graphene quantum dot-modified polyaniline for room-temperature sensing of alcohol vapors
coredata.dc:creator: Masemola, C.M.
Moloto, N.
Tetana, Z.N.
Gqoba, S.S.
Mubiayi, P.K.
Linganiso, E.C.
coredata.dc:description: Uniformly distributed nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were obtained using a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique. A composite of NGQDs and polyaniline (Pani) was prepared via an in situ chemical oxidative polymerization method and characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra. Room temperature operable gas sensors were prepared using bare Pani and NGQDs/Pani films deposited separately onto gold-plated interdigitated electrodes (IDEs) embedded on printed-circuit board (PCB) substrates. The prepared sensors showed a good response towards 50–150 ppm ethanol vapors at room temperatures and relative humidity of 45%. The incorporation of NGQDs in Pani promoted a better response signal and sensitivity, and improved the baseline resistance of the composites, which was attributed to the enhanced conductive pathways between Pani and NGQDs. The composite sensor reached the lowest response and recovery times of 85 s and 62 s towards 100 ppm of ethanol respectively. Both sensors exhibited a good reproducibility of the response signal towards 100 ppm of ethanol vapor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Polyaniline
Room temperature sensing
Alcohol sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of NGQDs synthesis via MW-HM and in-situ polymerization of NGQDs/Pani composite film.
Sensor fabrication of Pani and NGQDs/Pani film sensors.
Schematic illustration of a homemade gas sensing measurement setup.
TEM image of (a) NGQDs, (b) corresponding particle size distributions, (c & d) TEM images of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite.
XRD spectra of NGQDs, Pani, and NGQDs/Pani composite.
FTIR spectra of NGQDs, Pani, and NGQDs/Pani composite (left) with corresponding peak assignments (right).
(a) TGA and (b) DTG graphs of NGQDs, Pani, and NGQDs/Pani composite.
(a) Raman analysis of the NGQDs, Pani and NGQD/Pani samples. (b) XPS spectrum of NGQDs.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and their corresponding pore size distribution curves (insert) of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite.
UV–vis spectra of (a) NGQDs and (b) Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite.

(a) Gas response of Pani and NGQDs/Pani film sensors towards 50–150 ppm of ethanol at 26 °C in 45% RH, and (b) real time resistance change as a function of time for the NGQDs/Pani film sensor towards ethanol gas.
Gas response and recovery times of (a) Pani and (b) NGQDs/Pani under 100 ppm ethanol concentration.

(a) Dynamic responses of NGQDs/Pani sensor towards different concentrations of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol, and (b) response vs recovery times of NGQDs/Pani sensor towards 100 ppm of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol at 26 °C and 45% RH.
Response plot of (a) Pani, and (b–d) NGQDs/Pani composite sensors as a function of gas concentration.

head.ce:title: N-doped graphene quantum dot-modified polyaniline for room-temperature sensing of alcohol vapors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Microscopy and Microanalysis Unit, University of the Witwatersrand, Private Bag 3, Johannesburg, Wits, 2050, South Africa

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Molecular Sciences Institute, School of Chemistry, University of the Witwatersrand, Private Bag 3, Johannesburg, Wits, 2050, South Africa.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uniformly distributed nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were obtained using a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique. A composite of NGQDs and polyaniline (Pani) was prepared via an in situ chemical oxidative polymerization method and characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra. Room temperature operable gas sensors were prepared using bare Pani and NGQDs/Pani films deposited separately onto gold-plated interdigitated electrodes (IDEs) embedded on printed-circuit board (PCB) substrates. The prepared sensors showed a good response towards 50–150 ppm ethanol vapors at room temperatures and relative humidity of 45%. The incorporation of NGQDs in Pani promoted a better response signal and sensitivity, and improved the baseline resistance of the composites, which was attributed to the enhanced conductive pathways between Pani and NGQDs. The composite sensor reached the lowest response and recovery times of 85 s and 62 s towards 100 ppm of ethanol respectively. Both sensors exhibited a good reproducibility of the response signal towards 100 ppm of ethanol vapor.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) such as alcohols are used in many fields including pharmaceutical, printing, laboratories, food and medical industries. Continuous exposure to low concentrations of VOCs is known to cause serious health concerns to the human body [1]. For example, respiration disruption, headaches, loss of coordination, confusion, unconsciousness, loss of vision, and even death, can be caused by unlimited exposure to VOCs. Thus, developing fast, accurate, highly sensitive, and reliable low-cost chemiresistors aimed at monitoring low level concentrations (<200 ppm) of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol is of great significance [2].

Recently, sensor developers and material scientists are exploring new sensitive materials which can satisfy the demand of sensor/chemiresistor technology particularly, sensors operable at room temperature under normal atmospheric conditions are desirable [3,4]. Conjugated polymers such as polyaniline (Pani) have become increasingly popular for the design and development of the next-generation fuel cells, sensors, supercapacitors, rechargeable batteries, and corrosion resistant materials to list a few [5]. This is due to the advantage of low-cost monomer, tunable electrical conductivity, low operational voltage, and excellent environmental stability, for the Pani [5]. Its quick response/recovery times and good selectivity at room temperature has made it a desirable alternative to conventional sensors that require operational temperatures as high as 300 °C [4]. However, Pani is still restricted as a practical gas sensing material because of its long-term sensor drift, low thermal stability, low response, and its vulnerability to humidity.

To overcome these limitations, strategies to develop composites of Pani with other complementary gas sensing materials (including graphene-based carbon nanomaterials) have been adopted in an attempt to exploit associated advantages [5,6]. Some of the attempts by various researchers have significantly improved the sensing capabilities of Pani based composites, but its low response is yet to be solved. In a recent study on methanol detection by pure Pani and Pani/graphene composites performed at room temperature, Pal and colleagues reported a 42.5% increase in sensor response with the lowest response time of 55 s for 8 wt% doping of Pani with graphene [7]. Combining Pani with graphene-based carbon nanomaterials such as reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and graphene oxide (GO) has been shown to enhance sensor response towards ammonia gas at room temperature [8,9].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a class and a relatively new member of the graphene-based carbon nano-family, with lateral dimensions of less than 10 nm, are viewed as promising candidates for addition in polymer-based composites for gas sensors [10,11]. They combine the advantages of quantum dots and 2D graphene-like properties to offer special advantages such as tunable energy band gaps, high mechanical strength, distinctive photoluminescence, chemical stability, excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and excellent microelectronic properties owing to their quantum confinement effects and edge effects upon doping with various heteroatoms [10]. Very recently, there has been a growing interest in the preparation and application of GQDs in numerous advanced systems such as electrochemiluminescence sensors, nanofillers, biosensors, perovskite solar cells, lithium-ion batteries, supercapacitor, and optoelectronic devices. However, their application as gas sensitive materials for VOCs and other toxic gas analytes is still limited [10–13].

In this work, nitrogen doped GQDs (NGQDs) were synthesized following the bottom-up approach. A microwave-assisted hydrothermal method for ultra-fast fabrication of monodispoersed NGQDs was selected as a green and efficient synthesis approach [14]. The reduction of synthetic steps involved and the use of less toxic solvents saves time and minimizes hazardous waste production, respectively. The lack of reports on the application of NGQDs/Pani composites in the sensing of the selected alcohols at room temperature has motivated us to investigate this composite as a low cost alternative to Pani composites containing carbon nanotubes, graphene, reduced graphene oxide, etc. A composite of NGQDs/Pani was successfully applied as a flexible sensor for the detection of methanol, ethanol and isopropanol at room temperature.

Citric acid (monohydrate), urea, acetone, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, aniline monomer, hydrochloric acid, ammonium persulfate (APS), and ammonium hydroxide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (South Africa). All chemicals were used without further purification and distilled water was prepared in the lab with a pH of 6.

Synthesis of NGQDs was carried out using the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method (MW-HM). Briefly, 0.84 g (4 mmol) of citric acid (CA) and 0.72 g of urea (12 mmol) were mixed in 20 mL distilled water and stirred until a clear solution was obtained. Then, solution mixture was transferred into a MW oven and irradiated at 600 W for 4 min with 10 min ramping. The final product was collected under rotatory vapor at 75 °C. The synthesis procedure is schematically shown in Fig. 1 .

Rapid mixing polymerization method [15] was carried out by dissolving 1 mL of aniline monomer in 45 mL of 1 M hydrochloric acid (noted as A). To another beaker, 1.2 g of oxidant (APS) was dissolved in 45 mL 1 M hydrochloric acid and cooled to 4 °C (noted as B). Solution A was rapidly poured into solution B and mixed rigorously under magnetic stirring for 10 min. The resultant black Pani precipitate was then washed twice with distilled water to remove free acids and excess oxidant/aniline. Collected black-green Pani powder was further dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight.

In situ chemical polymerization method was carried out as follows: NGQDs were re-dispersed in 10 mL distilled water by sonication treatment for 30 min to form a solution concentration of 10 mg/mL (noted as A). To another beaker, 1 mL of aniline monomer, 1.2 g APS, and 90 mL hydrochloric acid (1 M) were added (noted as B) and mixed under magnetic stirring for 20 min. Solution B was then cooled to 4 °C using an ice bath. Solution A was added to solution B, mixed under constant stirring, and left to polymerize for 10 min. The resultant black precipitate was filtered and washed with distilled water to remove free acids or unreacted species. Collected black NGQDs/Pani powder was oven dried at 60 °C overnight. The synthesis procedure is schematically shown in Fig. 1.

The purity and crystallinity of as-prepared materials were examined using powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Cu-Kα (λ = 0.154 nm), Bruker-2D RXD) over a 2θ range of 10–70°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai T12) with accelerating voltage of 120 kV was used to analyze the surface morphologies and structure of as-prepared materials. Thermal gravity and differential scanning calorimeter (TG–DSC, PerkinElmer Pyris STA6000) were used to analyze the samples over a temperature range of 35–900 °C at an increment rate of 10 °C/min under a flowing air atmosphere. The specific surface areas and porosity measurements of Pani and NGQDs/Pani were carried out by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method using Micromeitics TriStar 3000 to outgas samples at 150 °C for 4 h. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Agilent Technologies Cary (100) Series) with a tungsten halogen light was used for light absorption properties excited at 350 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker TENSOR 27) scanned from 3500 to 600 cm−1 using 64 scans was used to examine the functional groups present on the surface structure of the materials. Finely ground Pani or NGQDs/Pani powders (40 mg each) were separately dissolved in distilled water and conductivity measurements were carried out using a Winlab Data Line Conductivity Meter at 25 °C.

A schematic illustration of a homemade gas sensing experimental setup is shown in Fig. 3. Sensing characteristics of the prepared sensors were investigated by exposing them to various vapor concentrations of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol at room temperature, while recording a change in sensor resistance over predetermined time intervals. In order to obtain desired concentrations in ppm, specific volumes (μL) of gas analytes were injected into a sealed test chamber (5 L) through the injection port using a 10 μL syringe. Vapor concentrations (ppm) inside the test chamber were calculated using equation (1) [16].
(1)
C
( p p m )
=
22.4 ρ T V s
273 MV
× 1000
where, C is the vapor concentration of gas analytes in ppm, V is the volume of a test chamber (L), Vs is the volume of gas analytes in liquid form (μL), ρ is the density of gas analytes in g/mL, T is the room temperature during testing (K), and M is the molar weight of gas analytes in g/mol.
The sensor response (S) was defined as: S (%) = (R a −R g)/R a) × 100%, where Ra and Rg were the sensor resistance in air and gas, respectively.

Fig. 4 a and b shows the TEM image of pure NGQDs and their corresponding diameter size distribution following the MW-HM. The as-prepared NGQDs demonstrate a well dispersed dot-like morphology of NGQDs with relatively uniform diameters of 5 ± 2.4 nm. This observation indicates that the MW-HM is an effective method for the synthesis of NGQDs that are similar to those previously synthesized by the electrochemical technique [17]. Fig. 4c and d shows the TEM images of pure Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite structures prepared via the rapid polymerization and in-situ rapid polymerization methods respectively. The images reveal a formation of 1D nanofibrous morphology with estimated fiber diameters of 41 ± 10 nm and 47 ± 14 nm for pure Pani and NGQDs/Pani respectively. The obtained fibers are smaller than previously reported (100 nm) Pani nanofibers synthesized under similar conditions [18]. From Fig. 4d, addition of NGQDs within Pani resulted in the formation of denser structures at the core, which can be related to the strong π-interactions between the NGQDs units and the aromatic rings on Pani.

Fig. 5 shows a typical XRD pattern for NGQDs, Pani, and NGQDs/Pani composite. The XRD profile for NGQDs shows a broad peak centered at ∼26°. This peak was assigned to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, revealing a graphitic nature of the NGQDs with highly disordered carbon atoms [19]. The spectra of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite show three characteristic peaks resembling a semicrystalline orthorhombic lattice structures matched to PDF 00-060-1167. The effect of NGQDs loading on Pani resulted in the formation of microstructures that reflects emeraldine salt conformations with enhanced peaks at 2θ ≈ 15.8°, 20.8°, and 25.4° corresponding to the (011), (020), (200) crystal planes [20]. The peaks centered at 2θ ≈ 20.8° are due to the inter-chain distance between benzene rings in parallel directions to Pani chains. The peaks centered at 2θ ≈ 25.4° are due to the periodicity of benzene rings perpendicular to Pani chains [21,22]. The absence of NGQDs peak in the NGQDs/Pani composite is due to the low concentration of NGQDs added. However, its structural influence is demonstrated in the XRD structure and TEM images of the composite. This implies that there are shorter inter-chain distances within Pani structures created by the inter-chain π-interaction between NGQDs nanosheets and Pani. This enhancement in microstructures shows that NGQDs/Pani composite has more ordered molecular arrangements, which may also improve the mobility of charge carriers [23].

Conductivity of 3.98 × 10−4 S cm−1 and 4.34 × 10−4 S cm−1 were recorded for bare Pani and NGQDs/Pani respectively. Distilled water had a measured electric conductivity of 1.56 × 10−6 S cm−1 which much lower than that of both samples. A recent report by Ref. [24] evaluated the effect of each type of charge to the total conductivity of Pani. Polarons, holes and ions were determined to have minimum contribution to the overall conductivity and the importance of the hydrogen and chlorine molecules for charge transfer was highlighted. Javadian-Saraf and colleagues [9] also highlighted in their report that Pani determines the conductivity of GO-Pani composites over the GO effect. The slight increase in the conductivity of the GQDs/Pani samples confirms that the GQDs improved the intra- and inter-chain conductivity of the Pani in agreement with the XRD data.

The FTIR spectrum of NGQDs show a strong broad band extending from 2800 to 3400 cm−1 which is related to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl group (O–H), amine group (N–H), and aromatic (=C–H) groups (Fig. 6 ). The presence of O–H group in this region can be associated with absorbed moisture since NGQDs are naturally hydroscopic [25,26]. This broad band is overlapping with the aromatic C–H usually found around 2800 cm−1 and this suggest the presence of sp3 carbons formed by the breaking of graphitic CC (sp2) carbons [31]. The strong absorption bands at 1650, 1548, and 1388 cm−1 are related to the vibrations in graphitic CC, NH stretching, and amide linkage vibrations in OC–NH, respectively. The band at 1191 cm−1 is ascribed to the alkoxy group in the NGQDs structure. The spectrum reflects high density of nitrogen groups, hydroxyl groups, and oxygenated carbon groups present on the edges of the NGQDs [27,28].

The FTIR spectra of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite is shown in Fig. 6. The spectra reflect bands at 2909 cm−1 ascribed to the C–H stretching vibrations of aromatic bonds caused by the Cl counter-ions. Two strong peaks at 1575 cm-1 and 1491 cm-1 region for aromatic ring vibrations, indicate the main characteristic peaks of Pani. These peaks are due to CN bonds in quinoids rings and CC bonds in benzenoid rings, respectively. The increased peak intensity for NGQDs/Pani composite at this region (1485 - 1580 cm−1) clearly demonstrates the interaction of NGQDs and Pani. The peaks at 1294 cm−1 are due to C–N stretching of aromatic amines in benzenoid units [29]. The peaks at 1130 and 795 cm−1 are ascribed to the in plane and out of plane distortion vibrations of the C–H bonds in benzene rings. The assignments of FT-IR peaks were verified by comparing with previous reports confirming the successful preparation of NGQDs/Pani composite [30,31].

The TGA and DTG thermograms of NGQDs, Pani, and NGQDs/Pani composite are presented in Fig. 7 a and b. The thermogram of NGQDs reveal four decomposition steps. In the first decomposition step, NGQDs incurred weight loss of 17% below 139 °C due to elimination of the moisture content [32]. At 223 °C, a drastic decomposition occurs with total weight loss of 46% due to elimination of amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups demonstrating high density of surface states [33]. At 424 °C, a weight loss of 21% is incurred due to the decomposition of phenyl groups or pyrrolidone and pyridine groups. The final decomposition occurs at 691 °C with weight loss of 36% due to oxidation of aromatic carbons into CO and/or CO2 gases [34,35].

The thermograms of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite reveal three decomposition steps. The first decomposition step below 100 °C is due to evaporation of absorbed moisture, where Pani and NGQDs/Pani incurred weight losses of 18% and 16%. The second decomposition step at 250 °C is due to elimination of dopants such as amines, hydroxyl, chloride, sulfur and oxygen containing compounds [36]. The samples incurred weight losses of 9% and 8% corresponding to Pani and NGQDs/Pani, respectively. The final decomposition step at 534 °C and 545 °C is due to the total degradation of the carbon backbone mostly constituting aromatic carbons from both NGQDs and Pani structures, which were converted into CO and/or CO2 gases [37]. The incurred weight losses of 73% and 76% corresponds to Pani and NGQDs/Pani. No additional decomposition step is observed after 545 °C and the samples reached a plateau. The TGA curve of NGQDs/Pani demonstrates an improved thermal stability compared to bare Pani owing to the intrinsic thermal characteristics of NGQDs.

The Raman spectra of the NGQDs, Pani, and NGQDs/Pani are shown in Fig. 8 a. The spectrum of NGQDs presents typical carbon characteristic peaks centered at 1327 and 1442 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands respectively. The peak intensity of the D-band is associated with the concentration of sp3 defects in the NGQDs structure and peak intensity of the G-band is related to the in-plane sp2 carbons [38]. The ratio of the two peaks ID/IG is used as a measure of the degree of disorder in the NGQDs structure. It was observed that NGQDs have an ID/IG ratio of ∼0.73 which is lower than that of pristine graphene ∼0.92 [39], signifying a disorder within NGQDs matrix due to defects. However, this value is similar to the NGQDs previously reported which were highly crystalline [40]. The spectrum of Pani shows bands located at 1600 and 1509 cm−1 which are related to the CC stretching of the benzenoid and quinoid rings. The band located at 1338 cm−1 dominates the spectrum and is linked with the C–N+· charge delocalization of polaron structures of the emeraldine salt Pani. The band at 1168 cm−1 is related with the C–H (in-plane) bending vibrations in quinoid and benzenoid units [23]. Other small bands at 806 and 573 cm−1 are connected to the benzene ring and amine (in-plane) deformation vibrations. For NGQDs/Pani, all characteristic peaks of Pani are visible [8]. The appearance of a doublet at 1376 cm−1 is linked with the CN stretching vibrations due to the contributions of the NGQDs. The intensity of the all the observable peaks are enhanced in NGQDs/Pani which demonstrates the growth of Pani and the interaction with NGQDs structures [23]. The survey XPS spectrum of the NGQDs is shown in Fig. 8b. The spectrum shows four peaks located at 533, 400, 285, and 100 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s and Si2p, respectively. The atomic % of each content was calculated to be 25% (O1s), 9.1% (N), 62% (C), and (Si2p) < 1% in which the spectrum demonstrated high density of oxygenated groups and amine functional groups [40].

The specific surface areas and porosity of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite were further investigated by measuring N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at −196 °C. As shown in Fig. 9 , the BET curves of the two samples presents type IV adsorption-isotherms which are characteristic of mesoporous structures with pore sizes concentrated in the ranges of about 30–45 nm (Fig. 9 insert). The presence of a hysteresis loop upon N2 desorption at a high relative pressure of approximately 0.95 is representative of type IV adsorption-isotherm with mesopores [41,42]. The BET surface areas of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite were calculated to be 31.2 and 41.0 m2 g−1, respectively.

Aqueous solution of NGQDs reflect a brown color under normal light while illuminating a bright blue fluorescent color under irradiation by UV lamp of 365 nm (16 W) as shown in Fig. 1. The UV–vis spectrum of the NGQDs is shown in Fig. 10 a. The spectrum shows a broad absorption peak centered at 330 nm corresponding to the optical transitions between n→π∗ of CN/CO bonds. This absorption is due to the excess of electrons in N and O atoms and thus contributes to the n-state transitions in CN/CO bonds [27,43]. The peak broadness can be attributed to a possible overlapping of the π→π∗ transitions in CC with the n→π∗ transition of the CN/CO bonds.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite are shown in Fig. 10b. The spectra reflect the absorption peaks: 288–330 nm (π→π∗ transitions in benzenoid rings), 448 nm (bi-polaron→π∗ transitions), and 650 nm (exciton of quinoid units). The absorption peaks are characteristic of Pani in the emeraldine salt form [31]. The interaction of NGQDs with Pani is clearly demonstrated in the spectra by the reduction of the peak at 288 nm causing an increase of the peaks at 330 nm and 650 nm due to the contribution of π→π∗ transitions of the CC bonds in NGQDs.

Fig. 11 a shows the gas response of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite sensors with the exposure to different concentrations of ethanol gas. Response measurements were performed at room temperature (26 °C) with ethanol gas concentrations ranging from 50 to 150 ppm under 45% RH. Each exposure/recovery cycle was carried out at predetermined time intervals of 300 s in gas followed by a recovery interval of 240 s in air. Both sensors demonstrate linear responses for increasing concentration of ethanol gas and at 100 ppm concentration, the response signal of pure Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite sensors is about 0.43% and 0.66%, respectively. In addition, the NGQDs/Pani composite sensor exhibits a much higher response by up to 39% compared to that of pure Pani for higher concentration of ethanol gas. This observation may be attributed to the presence of NGQDs that provided higher charge transfer pathways and contributed to increased surface area and absorption sites [44]. In summary, the NGQDs/Pani composite sensor is more selective to ethanol gas when compared to bare Pani.

Fig. 11b shows the real time resistance change as a function of time with exposure of NGQDs/Pani composite sensor towards 50–150 ppm of ethanol vapors (legal permissible exposure limit (PEL) is 1000 ppm in air). The response curve shows that, at lower ethanol concentrations, the response to ethanol was shown to increase until saturation was reached and a decrease back to the original baseline resistance was observed after the sensor was exposed to air. At higher concentrations, a delayed sensor's signal recovery was observed, which resulted in a slight increase of the baseline resistance.

Fig. 12 a and b shows response and recovery times of bare Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite sensors under exposure to 100 ppm of ethanol gas concentrations. The response and recovery times of Pani film sensor was found to be 118 s and 80 s, while the response and recovery times of NGQDs/Pani film sensor was 85 s and 62 s for 90% of response/recovery respectively.

Fig. 13 a plots the dynamic responses of the NGQDs/Pani film sensor to various concentrations of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol in the range of 50–150 ppm at 26 °C in 45% RH, respectively. The sensor exhibits linear responses for increasing concentrations of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol in the range of 50–150 ppm, demonstrating the dependence of response on the concentration of gas molecules. This observation may be attributed to additional gas molecules absorbed on available active surface of the sensing layer, which results in increased response [45]. Fig. 13b shows response and recovery times of the NGQDs/Pani sensor toward 100 ppm of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol vapors. The measured percentage response and response times to 100 ppm concentration of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol were recorded to be 0.15% (100 s), 0.66% (85 s), and 0.80% (132 s), respectively. The corresponding recovery times of 96 s, 62 s, and 78 s were recorded for methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol respectively. Furthermore, the sensor showed a stable linear increase in response for about 80–98 s, after which it started decreasing with further increase in time indicating that a saturation level was reached. This can be attributed to the reduced available surface adsorption sites required for diffusion of vapor analytes [12,45].

The linear relationship between gas response and gas concentration was used to generate linear fitted plots of response vs concentration of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol as shown in Fig. 14 a–d. These plots can be used to monitor the concentration of the applied alcohol vapors on the sensor film. The sensitivity of a sensor is defined as ‘the change of a measured signal per analyte concentration unit’, which was estimated by measuring the slopes of the fitted plots. Therefore, the observed sensitivities of 0.00313, 0.00519, 0.00124, and 0.00511 ppm−1 were determined for Pani (ethanol) and NGQDs/Pani (ethanol, methanol, isopropanol), respectively. This shows that even though isopropanol produced the highest response signal, the highest sensitivity was recorded for ethanol, over the measured gas concentration range. This may be related with the geometry and binding energies of VOCs, which were previously reported to play a vital role on gas sensitivity [46].

Fig. S1a-d demonstrates the reproducibility of the prepared sensors toward 100 ppm of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol gases. The reproducibility characteristic was evaluated for four repeated response/recovery cycles. The prepared sensors shows a clear response/recovery performance and achieved stable reproducibility for four repeated cycles. This is due to the unique protonation/deprotonation characteristics of Pani which allows it to achieve a stable baseline after each gas/air exposure cycle [12,47]. The reproducibility study shows that the designed sensors are stable and can be suitable candidates for practical applications.

In summary, the fabrication of Pani and NGQDs/Pani composite sensors for room-temperature alcohol gas sensing was successfully demonstrated. The synthesis of NGQDs/Pani composite was carried out by in-situ polymerization method and characterized using TEM, XRD, FT-IR, BET, TGA, and UV–Vis spectra. The prepared NGQDs/Pani composite exhibited a higher BET specific surface area when compared to bare Pani and the XRD data confirmed that the composite material had an improved crystallinity. This was attributed to the induced conductive pathways created by strong synergetic effects through inter-chain π interactions between NGQDs and Pani as confirmed by the conductivity measurements. The properties of the prepared sensors were investigated for various concentrations of methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol ranging from 50 to 150 ppm at 26 °C in 45% RH. Both sensors demonstrated a good reproducibility of the sensing signal when exposed to 100 ppm ethanol vapors for up to four cycles. The NGQDs/Pani composite sensor presented a better response signal of 0.66%, 23% higher compared to that of bare Pani, and achieved the fastest response-recovery time of 85 s and 62 s towards 100 ppm ethanol vapor. This was attributed to its high surface porosity which enabled easy adsorption and desorption of the analyte gas. Our sensors have demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity towards isopropanol and ethanol gases. Future studies should evaluate the selectivity of this composite to various VOCs, effect of humidity and GQDs concentration on Pani.

C.M. Masemola: was involved in the writing of the original draft and methodology design. N. Moloto: was involved in the supervision of the project. Z.N. Tetana: was involved in the supervision of the project. S.S. Gqoba: was involved in the supervision of the project. P.K. Mubiayi: was involved in the supervision of the project. E.C. Linganiso: was involved in the supervision of the project, Conceptualization, and review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support received from NRF Thuthuka (grant number 129532) and the DSI/CoE in Strong Materials, University of the Witwatersrand.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2022.126229.
